sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Combat Application Tourniquet (CAT) is the most widely used military and prehospital tourniquet for life-threatening limb haemorrhage. Early tourniquet application before haemorrhagic shock occurs is associated with significantly improved survival.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
**Self-application / one-handed:**

1. Route limb through loop; position 5–7 cm above wound (or as high as possible for junctional injuries)

2. Pull free end of self-adhering band tight; secure

3. Twist windlass until bleeding stops (typically 3–5 turns)

4. Lock windlass in clip; secure tail strap over windlass

5. Note time of application on white label on windlass clip

**Two-handed application:**

1. Open CAT; pass limb through loop

2. Position 5–7 cm above wound

3. Tighten band by pulling free end; secure

4. Twist windlass until bleeding stops; lock and secure

5. Mark time

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Apply tight enough to stop bleeding — partial occlusion worsens blood loss and increases pain without benefit
- Effective tourniquet is painful — reassure patient
- Do not remove prehospital — hospital will assess and manage
- Mark time of application on tourniquet and in patient record
- Maximum recommended application time before ischaemic injury risk increases: up to 2 hours (survival benefit outweighs limb risk)
